title: Why Fast R-CNN?
concept: Fast R-CNN accelerates detection by processing the entire image through the CNN backbone once to generate a shared feature map.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main speed bottleneck in the original R-CNN?

options.text: Running the CNN 2,000 times per image on cropped regions

exercises.explain: Evaluating the CNN 2,000 times for each proposed crop is R-CNN's main bottleneck.

cards.title: Sharing the Workload
cards.body: If 10 classmates want to write down notes from a whiteboard, they don't take turns copying the board one by one. One student takes a photo of the board and shares it with the group, so everyone can extract the parts they need. That is the philosophy of Fast R-CNN.

cards.title: Fast R-CNN
cards.body: Fast R-CNN is an upgraded object detection network. Instead of cropping the image and running the CNN 2,000 times, it passes the entire image through the CNN backbone once to produce a shared feature map, significantly reducing computation.

cards.title: One Large Map vs. 2000 Snippets
cards.body: Think of drawing a large, detailed map of a city once. Anyone looking for specific neighborhoods just looks at their section of the shared map, rather than asking 2,000 separate cartographers to draw individual sketches of each street.

cards.title: Speedup Stats
cards.body: Fast R-CNN processes images 100 times faster than R-CNN during inference, and trains 9 times faster, eliminating the need to store gigabytes of features on disk.

cards.title: Still uses Selective Search
cards.body: It's a myth that Fast R-CNN does not use Selective Search. It still relies on Selective Search to generate region proposals; it just projects those proposals onto the shared feature map instead of cropping the raw image.

cards.title: R-CNN vs. Fast R-CNN
cards.body: Comparing feature extraction between R-CNN and Fast R-CNN:

right.text:
Entire image -> 1 CNN run -> Shared map.
Takes 0.3 seconds (excluding proposals).

exercises.prompt: How does Fast R-CNN solve R-CNN's speed problem?
exercises.explain: Fast R-CNN extracts features from the entire image once, allowing multiple regions to share the computation.

options.text: By running the CNN backbone once on the entire image to generate a shared feature map
By removing the CNN backbone entirely
By running Selective Search on a GPU

exercises.prompt: True or False: Fast R-CNN requires storing extracted feature vectors on disk during training.
exercises.explain: False. Fast R-CNN is trained end-to-end in a single stage, meaning features do not need to be saved to disk.
Only one forward pass is run on the whole image to extract the shared feature map.
exercises.sentence: Fast R-CNN passes the entire image through the CNN ___ time(s) to create a shared feature map.

exercises.prompt: Which feature is associated with Fast R-CNN?
exercises.explain: Sharing the feature map across all proposals is Fast R-CNN's primary feature.

options.text: Using a shared convolutional feature map to evaluate region proposals
Removing the region proposal step entirely

exercises.prompt: You are upgrading an old object detection service from R-CNN to Fast R-CNN. How does this impact your storage and hardware costs during training?

options.text: It reduces storage costs, because you no longer need to save 2,000 feature vectors per image to disk, and reduces compute costs by sharing features.
It increases storage costs, because shared feature maps are larger than raw images.
It has no impact on training.
It requires buying expensive CPU licenses.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The shared feature map and end-to-end training pipeline eliminate both the storage nightmare and redundant GPU runs. Next: let's learn how we project proposals onto these shared feature maps!
